title: בְּלוּלֵי אֵשׁ וּמֵימוֹת | Blulei Esh uMeimot — an ofan piyyut for Shabbat haGadol by Rabbi Binyamin bar Zeraḥ
author: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (transcription & naqdanut)

coauthors.name: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (transcription & naqdanut)
coauthors.bio: From a family of musicians, Isaac Gantwerk Mayer believes that creative art is one of the most powerful ways to get in touch with the divine. He composes music and poetry in Hebrew and English. (He also translates and authors his own original works.) Isaac runs a Jewish music transcription service, which will transcribe and set any Jewish music in any language, recorded or written. Contact his service on Facebook or via his music blog.

coauthors.name: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (translation)
coauthors.bio: From a family of musicians, Isaac Gantwerk Mayer believes that creative art is one of the most powerful ways to get in touch with the divine. He composes music and poetry in Hebrew and English. (He also authors his own original works and transcribes Hebrew and Aramaic text, adding niqqud and t'amim as needed.) Isaac runs a Jewish music transcription service, which will transcribe and set any Jewish music in any language, recorded or written. Contact his service on Facebook or via his music blog.

coauthors.name: Binyamin bar Zeraḥ
coauthors.bio: Benjamin ben Zerah (Hebrew: בנימין בן זרח) was a paytan who lived in southeastern Europe in the middle of the eleventh century. He is called by the later payyetanim "the Great," and also "Ba'al Shem" (Master of the Name), on account of the numerous names of God and angels used by him in his piyyutim. He wrote 15 poems ("yotsrot") for the Sabbaths preceding the feasts, and 40 seliḥot, published in the Maḥzor of the German rite. His piyyutim have an easy, elegant style. Parallels with Eleazar beRabbi Qallir are frequent. Judging from his seliḥah אנא ה' האל ("I beseech thee, Hashem ha-El) – in which he plays on the name of God – consisting of 22 letters, and his "ofan," in which he gives the names of angels, Benjamin was inclined to mysticism.

excerpt: An ofan (a piyyut inserted into the Qedusha d'Yotser) recited in the eastern Ashkenazi Minhag Polin rite for Shabbat haGadol. Signed "Benjamin," Davidson ascribes it to the great paytan R. Binyamin bar Zeraḥ, possibly as part of the same poetic cycle as Itti MiLvanon (his yotser for the same day).
content:
An ofan (a piyyut inserted into the Qedusha d’Yotser) recited in the eastern Ashkenazi Minhag Polin rite for Shabbat haGadol. Signed “Benjamin,” Davidson ascribes it to the great paytan R. Binyamin bar Zeraḥ, possibly as part of the same poetic cycle as Itti MiLvanon (his yotser for the same day).
Source (Hebrew) Source (English)
בְּלוּלֵי אֵשׁ וּמֵימוֹת
גִּבּוֹרֵי עִיר מְרוֹמוֹת
נִצָּבִים כַּחוֹמוֹת
בִּרְתָתִים וְאֵימוֹת
מַרְבִּים תַּעֲצוּמוֹת
לָאֵל יוֹסֵד הֲדוֹמוֹת
וַאֲנִי אֹם מֵאֻמּוֹת
בְּחֵיךְ וְגָרוֹן רוֹמֵמוֹת׃
Blended from fire and water,
exulting are the great watcher-angels cheer;
standing like walls
in terror and awe,
emphasizing the potency of the Deity,
establisher of footstools.
And as for me, the nation of nations
cheers with palate and throat.
נוֹהֵם מִיכָאֵל
וְגוֹעֶה גַּבְרִיאֵל
זוֹעֵק אוּרִיאֵל
וְגוֹעֵשׁ רְפָאֵל
וּמְהַדֵּר הֲדַרְנִיאֵל
הוֹד לְיָהּ אַכְתְּרִיאֵל
וַאֲנִי כְּנֶֽסֶת יִשְׂרָאֵל
מְעִידָה כִּי אֵין כָּאֵל׃
Now Michael[1] Literally: “Who is like the Deity?”Israel’s military advocate, and one of two angels mentioned by name in the book of Daniel.  roars,
and Gabriel[2] Literally: “Might of the Deity.” The revealer of hidden wisdom, and the other of two angels mentioned by name in the book of Daniel  lows,
and Uriel[3] Literally: “Light of the Deity.” The organizer of the hosts of heaven in their arrays. First mentioned in I Enoch.  screams,
and Raphael[4] Literally: “Healer is the Deity.” The bringer of healing. First mentioned in Tobit.  surges,
and Hadarniel[5] Literally: “Surrounding the Deity.” Six hundred thousand parasangs taller than his companions. First mentioned in Pesiqta Rabbati.  resplends
splendor to Yah Akhtriel.[6] Literally: “Yah, the crowned Deity.” The divine presence. First mentioned by this name in Berakhot 7a:4 
And as for me, the congregation of Israel
testifies that none is like the Deity El.
יְרוֹצְצוּ כִּבְרָקִים
חִיל זְוָעוֹת וְזִקִּים
לַפִּידִים דּוֹלְקִים
וְכַבָּזָק בּוֹזְקִים
תֻּשְׁבָּחוֹת מְפִיקִים
וְשִׁעוּר רֹן מְדַקְדְּקִים
וַאֲנִי שׁוֹשַׁנַּת הָעֲמָקִים
עֲסוּקָה בְּמִצְוֺת וְחֻקִּים׃
Jolting forth like lightning,
a force of trembling and glinting,
torches burning,
like sparks sparking;
bringing out laudations,
carefully ordering cheer.
And as for me, the lily of the valley
delves into the commandments and laws.
מְאַת אַלְפֵי אֲלָפִים רְבָבוֹת
מַרְכְּבוֹת אֵשׁ וְשַׁלְהֵבוֹת
מַקִּיפוֹת וְסוֹבְבוֹת
עִלּוּי כֵּס עֲרָבוֹת
שָׁלֹשׁ קְדֻשּׁוֹת דּוֹבְבוֹת
וְקוֹל דְּמָמָה מַקְשִׁיבוֹת
וַאֲנִי בַּת שְׁלֹֽשֶׁת אָבוֹת
תָּמִיד תְּפִלּוֹתַי עֲרֵבוֹת׃
Myriad hundred thousand thousands
of chariots of fire and ignition,
encircling and surrounding
the highest cloud-throne,
uttering the trisagion
and listening to the still small voice.
And as for me, the daughter of three patriarchs,
may my prayers always be pleasant!
נַהֲרֵי אֵימָה וְחִילָה
זֵעַת חַיּוֹת מַגְדִּילָה
וְעַל־אַרְבַּעְתָּן מִלְמַֽעְלָה
חַיָּה חֲמִישִׁית גְּדוֹלָה
לְסַלְסֵל הִיא מַתְחִילָה
וְאַחֲרֶֽיהָ פַּמַּֽלְיָא כֻלָּהּ
וַאֲנִי בְּבֵית הַתְּפִלָּה
תַּבַּֽעְנָה שְׂפָתַי תְּהִלָּה׃
Nullahs of awe and dread,
the sweat of the beast-angels swells them;
and over the four
the fifth beast-angel is greatest.
It starts to trill,
and after that the whole ménage.
And as for me, in the house of prayer,
praise flows from my lips.
וְהַחַיּוֹת יְשׁוֹרֵֽרוּ
וּכְרוּבִים יְפָאֵֽרוּ
וּשְׂרָפִים יָרֹֽנּוּ
וְאֶרְאֶלִּים יְבָרֵֽכוּ
פְּנֵי כׇּל־חַיָּה
וְאוֹפָן וּכְרוּב
לְעֻמַּת שְׂרָפִים
לְעֻמָּתָם מְשַׁבְּחִים וְאוֹמְרִים׃
בָּר֥וּךְ כְּבוֹד־יְהֹוָ֖ה
מִמְּקוֹמֽוֹ׃ (יחזקאל גֲ:יב)
And the beast-angels sing
and the mount-angels glorify
and the snake-angels cheer
and the lion-angels bless
before each beast-angel
and wheel-angel and mount-angel
facing the snake-angels,
facing them, lauding and saying:
Blessed be the Cause’s glory
from its place! (Ezekiel 3:12)

 

Notes

Notes
1 Literally: “Who is like the Deity?”Israel’s military advocate, and one of two angels mentioned by name in the book of Daniel.
2 Literally: “Might of the Deity.” The revealer of hidden wisdom, and the other of two angels mentioned by name in the book of Daniel
3 Literally: “Light of the Deity.” The organizer of the hosts of heaven in their arrays. First mentioned in I Enoch.
4 Literally: “Healer is the Deity.” The bringer of healing. First mentioned in Tobit.
5 Literally: “Surrounding the Deity.” Six hundred thousand parasangs taller than his companions. First mentioned in Pesiqta Rabbati.
6 Literally: “Yah, the crowned Deity.” The divine presence. First mentioned by this name in Berakhot 7a:4
